title: Beef chilli
subtitle: Texas-style beef and kidney bean chilli with a proper spice base
excerpt: A deep, dark chilli made with beef mince and kidney beans in a sauce built from dried chillies, cumin, garlic, and tinned tomatoes. Simmered for at least an hour until the fat has risen and the sauce has thickened to a deep, brick-red stew. Better the next day. Served with soured cream, cheddar, and cornbread or rice.
sourceNotes: Chilli con carne has its origin in the cooking of the Tex-Mex border region, with the first documented references appearing in San Antonio in the mid-nineteenth century. The preparation spread nationally through the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, appearing in American cookbooks and restaurant menus across the country. USDA Extension Service publications on Southwestern cooking from the early twentieth century document similar preparations.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well for up to 3 months. Reheat from frozen in a covered saucepan over low heat, adding a splash of water if needed.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or triples easily. The flavour improves with age.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the day after it is made. Refrigerate overnight and reheat gently.

content.text: Good chilli requires a proper spice base — not a single tablespoon of 'chilli powder' from a packet. The flavour comes from the combination of cumin, smoked paprika, dried oregano, cayenne, and if possible a dried ancho or pasilla chilli soaked and blended into the sauce. Toast the whole spices briefly in the dry pan before the meat goes in: the heat blooms the volatile oils and the difference in the finished dish is noticeable.

Cook it for at least an hour after the tomatoes and beans go in. The 30-minute version is a stew; the 75-minute version is a chilli. The fat that rises to the surface by the end of the cooking time can be skimmed off or stirred back in — the choice is textural as much as dietary. Season at the very end, not throughout: the reduction concentrates salt.

items.prepNote: 70% minimum, stirred in at the end

content.text: Heat a little oil in the saucepan over medium-high heat. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and cook, breaking it up, for 6 to 8 minutes until deeply browned. Remove to a plate. In the same pan, add the {{onion}} and {{red-pepper}} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and the spice base: {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika}} of paprika, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, {{dried-oregano}} of dried oregano, and {{cayenne}} of cayenne. Stir for 1 to 2 minutes until fragrant.

Return the beef to the pan. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans, and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Bring to a simmer, then cook uncovered on a low heat for at least 1 hour, stirring occasionally, until dark, thick, and reduced. If using dark chocolate, stir in the {{dark-chocolate}} now and stir to melt. Season generously with salt and taste — adjust the heat level with more cayenne or chilli flakes if needed.

items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered; the liquid reduces significantly over 60 to 75 minutes — stir occasionally and check the level

items.cause: Spice base not properly toasted, or insufficient spice quantities
items.fix: The ground spices must be cooked in the oil before the liquid goes in — raw ground spice has a dusty flavour; toast for a full 2 minutes

items.symptom: Too much surface fat by the end of cooking
items.cause: High-fat mince rendering throughout the cook
items.fix: Skim with a spoon, or refrigerate overnight — the fat will solidify on the surface and lift off cleanly

items.symptom: Chilli is too salty after reducing
items.cause: Seasoned at the start rather than the end
items.fix: Season only in the final 10 minutes, after the reduction is largely complete; a stock-based sauce concentrates salt as it reduces

content.text: Chilli has its origins in the Tex-Mex cooking tradition of the Texas-Mexico border, where the preparation of dried chillies with beef was documented in San Antonio as early as the 1840s. The 'chilli queens' of San Antonio's Military Plaza were famous for their outdoor stalls serving chilli con carne by the 1880s, and the dish spread nationally through the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, becoming one of the most widely cooked dishes in the American home kitchen. It is one of those preparations where intense regional identity (Texas purists exclude beans; Kansas City includes them) coexists with broad national adoption, and every household has its version.
